coredata.dc:title: Concentration-dependent and color-tunable room temperature afterglow of carbon dots@boric acid composites

coredata.dc:description: Color-tunable room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials have become excellent candidates for a wide range of applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. However, the achievement of color-tunable afterglow materials in a simple way remains a considerable challenge. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention in preparing metal-free long afterglow materials. Herein, a convenient and low-cost strategy with different doping concentrations for the preparation of carbon dots@boric acid (CDs@BA) composites with ultralong lifetime RTA has been proposed. Excitingly, the CDs@BA-(Ⅰ, Ⅱ, Ⅲ and Ⅳ) composites not only have ultralong afterglow lifetimes, but also exhibit color-tunable properties ranging from turquoise to deep yellow afterglow. In addition, these CDs@BA composites have been successfully used in the optical pattern design of safety exit signs and hazardous material signs. These findings may provide a new approach for the design of CDs-based nanomaterials with ultralong and color-tunable afterglow under ambient conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature afterglow
Concentration-dependence
Color-tunability

The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis and concentration-dependent fluorescence characteristics of seed-CDs. (b) Normalized UV–visible absorption spectrum of seed-CDs aqueous solution (1.56 mg/mL) and absorption difference spectra (ΔA) of 6.25–1.56, 25.00–6.25 and 50.00–25.00 mg/mL of seed-CDs aqueous solutions. (c) Normalized fluorescence spectra of seed-CDs aqueous solutions with different concentrations (1.56, 6.25, 25.00 and 50.00 mg/mL) excited by excitation-wavelength of 365 nm.

(a) TEM image of seed-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution diagrams of seed-CDs. (c) HR-TEM image of seed-CDs. (d) XRD pattern of seed-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectrum of seed-CDs. (f) Full scan XPS spectrum of seed-CDs. (g–i) High resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of seed-CDs.
Illustration of CDs@BA-(Ⅰ-Ⅳ) synthesis route, and images of CDs@BA-(Ⅰ-Ⅳ) captured with a UV lamp (365 nm) on or off, respectively.

(a–d) The fluorescence and phosphorescence emission spectra of CDs@BA-Ⅰ, CDs@BA-Ⅱ, CDs@BA-Ⅲ and CDs@BA-Ⅳ powders, respectively. (e–h) The room temperature phosphorescence decay spectra of CDs@BA-Ⅰ, CDs@BA-Ⅱ, CDs@BA-Ⅲ and CDs@BA-Ⅳ powders, respectively.

(a) Photographs of CDs@BA-Ⅰ and CDs@BA-Ⅲ powders designing “safety exit” and “hazardous material” patterns captured with a UV lamp (365 nm) on or off, respectively. (b) The optical images of CDs@BA-Ⅲ powders (marked symbol “I”), seed-CDs aqueous solution (1.56 mg/mL, marked symbol “♥”) and CDs@BA-Ⅲ powders (marked symbol “U”) captured with a UV lamp (365 nm) on or off, respectively.

head.ce:title: Concentration-dependent and color-tunable room temperature afterglow of carbon dots@boric acid composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi, 046011, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Changzhi University, Changzhi 046011, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environment, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environment, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei 230036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Color-tunable room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials have become excellent candidates for a wide range of applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. However, the achievement of color-tunable afterglow materials in a simple way remains a considerable challenge. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted more and more attention in preparing metal-free long afterglow materials. Herein, a convenient and low-cost strategy with different doping concentrations for the preparation of carbon dots@boric acid (CDs@BA) composites with ultralong lifetime RTA has been proposed. Excitingly, the CDs@BA-(Ⅰ, Ⅱ, Ⅲ and Ⅳ) composites not only have ultralong afterglow lifetimes, but also exhibit color-tunable properties ranging from turquoise to deep yellow afterglow. In addition, these CDs@BA composites have been successfully used in the optical pattern design of safety exit signs and hazardous material signs. These findings may provide a new approach for the design of CDs-based nanomaterials with ultralong and color-tunable afterglow under ambient conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials, including room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) materials, are widely used in biomedical imaging, anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronic devices, molecular switches and sensors [1–6]. However, achieving afterglow luminescent materials is a huge challenge because the afterglow emission process involves the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions [5,7,8]. Up to date, many afterglow luminescent materials are organometallic complexes and metal inorganic salts, but these systems often suffer from potential toxicity, high cost and not easy to manufacture [9–12]. Hence, it is very desirable to construct new groups of metal-free materials (including pure organic and nano systems) with a long lifetime, low-cost and environmentally friendly features through simple methods.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), as a new kind of optical nanomaterial, have attracted a lot of research attention due to easy synthesis, abundant surface functional groups, low toxicity, biocompatibility and superior optical and electrical properties [13–16]. Among them, long-afterglow CDs exhibit particularly superior potential over fluorescent CDs in applications such as information encryption, optoelectronic devices and bioimaging, owing to their long-lived luminescence and minimal background interference [13]. However, the majority of the publications on CDs have concentrated on various fluorescence (FL) properties, and there are few reports on afterglow optical properties. According to previous reports, there are two primary methods for CDs to achieve afterglow emission: (I) Design of new type of CDs with polymer-like structures, named carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [7,12,17–20]. For example, Lin et al. designed a simple method for the preparation of ultralong RTP CDs with polymer-like structures through microwave-assisted heating of ethanolamine and phosphoric acid aqueous solution [7]. Yang et al. developed a hydrothermal addition polymerization and carbonization (HAPC) strategy to prepare ultrahigh-yield acrylamide-based N-doped carbonized polymer dots (AN-CPDs) with a tunable RTP lifetime and wavelength [12]. (II) The CDs are embedded or immobilized in certain and suitable solid matrices [5,8,11,21–26]. For example, Hu et al. designed a universal and effective approach to activate the RTP of CDs in boric acid (BA) matrix [11]. Shi et al. presented a basic design rule for achieving of RTP with life expectancy and efficiency enhancement by taking advantage of the defects from inorganic layer materials [24]. Yu et al. proposed a dots-in-zeolites technology to synthesize CD-based composites with interesting TADF properties with ultralong lifetimes [26].

Among various solid matrices, BA has garnered significant attention due to its numerous advantages, including its remarkable structural simplicity, stability and abundance of active sites [27–39]. These attributes effectively mitigate the non-radiative transition of guest triplet excitons resulting from molecular vibrations. In addition, the choice of CDs is another factor that cannot be ignored. Concentration-dependent CDs have attracted our interest, the advantage of such CDs is that they correspond to different FL emission wavelengths at different concentrations [40–46]. This interesting phenomenon may provide the possibility to manufacture tunable afterglow CDs-based BA materials (CDs@BA) with different doping concentrations.

In this work, we innovatively developed a multi-color afterglow regulation system based on a single CDs. Through a concentration-dependent doping strategy, we successfully prepared CDs@BA-(Ⅰ, Ⅱ, Ⅲ and Ⅳ) composites with tunable afterglow colors using diethylenetriamine, L-aspartic acid and BA as precursors. Firstly, a one-pot hydrothermal method was employed to treat L-aspartic acid and diethylenetriamine to prepare concentration-dependent and FL-tunable seed-CDs. Subsequently, a series of CDs@BA composites were synthesized through a thermal melting process, using seed-CDs with varying doping amounts and the fixed quantity of BA, respectively. During thermal treatment progress, seed-CDs were incorporated into BA powders, which could restrain non-radiative relaxation through locking the triplet exciton and inhibiting the intermolecular collision. As the doping concentration of CDs increases, the afterglow color shifts continuously from turquoise to deep yellow. As far as we know, this is a relatively rare phenomenon in CDs-based afterglow materials. Based on the afterglow phenomenon of CDs@BA composites, afterglow patterns can be designed for safety exit signs, hazardous material signs, and other intriguing optical patterns, demonstrating significant potential for low-cost and ease of manufacturing.

As displayed in Fig. 1 a, diethylenetriamine and L-Aspartic acid were selected to synthesize seed-CDs with hydrothermal treatment kept at 200 °C for 10 h in aqueous solution. The synthesized seed-CDs exhibit obvious optical tunability at different concentrations. The optical properties of seed-CDs in water were determined by UV–Vis and fluorescent spectroscopies (Fig. 1b and c). The UV–Vis absorption of the seed-CDs in low concentration (1.56 mg/mL) has two peaks at 210 and 239 nm due to the π-π∗ transitions, and 343 nm is attributed to the n-π∗ transitions [31], while the new absorption peaks appear at 376 nm (6.25 mg/mL), 433 nm (25.00 mg/mL) and 476 nm (50.00 mg/mL) as the concentration increases (Fig. 1b). Similar to most other reported CDs, the synthesized seed-CDs also exhibit concentration-dependent emission behavior [40–46]. As illustrated in Fig. 1c, the FL emission peaks of seed-CDs gradually shift from 449 to 548 nm under 365 nm excitation as the concentration of seed-CDs aqueous solution increases from 1.56 mg/mL to 50.00 mg/mL. The observed phenomena may be attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [47]. The comparative analysis of spectral overlap shows that the absorption spectra of the solutions with higher concentrations (6.25, 25.00 and 50.00 mg/mL) and the emission spectra of the diluted solutions (1.56 mg/mL) show increasingly larger overlaps, indicating concentration-dependent FRET effect enhancement (Fig. S1).

The morphology and structure of the carbon nanoparticles were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy for confirmation. As shown in Fig. 2a, S2a and 2b, the TEM images and size distribution histogram of seed-CDs reveal that the CDs are quasi-spherical particles with good dispersion, with an average size of 1.72 nm. Using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is observed, which aligns with the (100) plane of graphite, suggesting that the CDs contain a minor proportion of graphite-like formations (Fig. 2c and S2b) [48]. In Fig. 2d, the broad peak centered at approximately 21° on the XRD pattern spectrum is indicative of a structure characterized by disorder and amorphousness [48].

To explore the chemical combinations of seed-CDs, fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed. As depicted in Fig. 2e, a comparatively wide absorption peak appears at 3261 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H bonds. The peaks noticed at 2931 and 2864 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration modes of C-H bonds. The absorption bands indicative of C=O and C=N stretching vibrations were detected approximately at 1775 and 1635 cm−1, respectively, whereas the stretching vibrations of C=C and C-N were detected at 1559 and 1373 cm−1 [40]. The peak observed at 1183 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-O bonds, suggests a notable concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups within the structure. In Fig. 2f, the XPS full scan spectrum of the seed-CDs verifies the presence of all relevant elements, including C, N and O. In addition, the calculated atomic percentages are as follows: 65.90 % for the C element, 14.91 % for the N element and 19.19 % for the O element, respectively (Table S1). As shown in Fig. 2g, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s reveals three peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.3 eV and 287.2 eV, which are assigned to C=C/C-C, C-O/C-N and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively [49,50]. The XPS spectrum of N 1s can be decomposed into two peaks corresponding to C-N=C at 399.2 eV and N-H at 400.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2h) [50]. As shown in Fig. 2i, the O 1 spectrum can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 530.3 eV, 531.2 eV and 532.4 eV, corresponding to C=O, C-O/O-H and O-C=O bonds, respectively [50]. The results suggest that the seed-CDs are doped with C, N and O elements, aligning with the results obtained from the FT-IR spectrum. Based on the analysis of optical and structural properties, the seed-CDs can act as dopants and play the role of luminescent centers in the room temperature afterglow (RTA) system.

As depicted in Fig. 3 , after heating treatment, the seed-CDs are successfully embedded into the BA powders (the detailed experiment process is shown in supporting information). As expected, the products (CDs@BA-Ⅰ, CDs@BA-Ⅱ, CDs@BA-Ⅲ and CDs@BA-Ⅳ) synthesized using BA (3.0 g) with varying amounts of seed-CDs (4.8 mg, 18.8 mg, 75.0 mg, and 150.0 mg) display weak fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp excitation and exhibit longtime RTP emission after the light is turned off. Notably, these products exhibit weak fluorescence and obvious afterglow emissions that depend on the doping concentration of CDs (Fig. 3). Specifically, the phosphorescence spectrum gradually red-shifts as the CDs doping ratio in the BA matrix increases (Fig. S3). The RTP quantum yields (QYs) of CDs@BA composites were further determined to be 3.14 %, 1.72 %, 1.53 % and 0.70 % for CDs@BA-Ⅰ, CDs@BA-Ⅱ, CDs@BA-Ⅲ and CDs@BA-Ⅳ under 365 nm excitation, respectively (Fig. S4).

The FL spectra (black lines) of these CDs@BA composites are shown in Fig. 4 a–d. Interestingly enough, the FL spectra of seed-CDs aqueous solution with different concentrations show single emission at 365 nm excitation (Fig. 1c), while the FL spectra of CDs@BA-(Ⅰ-Ⅳ) composites show double emission peaks at 365 nm excitation (Fig. 4a–d). When seed-CDs were embedded in the BA matrix, the interaction between CDs and the matrix cannot be ignored. BA can passivate the surface functional groups of CDs, which may induce the formation of dual emission centers in CDs@BA-(I-IV) composites [30,31]. Specifically, the FL spectra of these CDs@BA composites excited at 365 nm featured two emission bands peaking at around 434 and 466 nm. As shown in Fig. S5, with the increase of CDs doping concentration, the enhanced tail at longer wavelengths becomes more obvious, which may be due to the aggregation of CDs caused by the increased CDs concentration. Meanwhile, as the amount of CDs increases from 4.8 to 150.0 mg, the ratio of the FL emission intensity of the second emission band to that of the first emission band gradually increases from 0.736 to 1.061. Moreover, the relevant RTP peak exhibits a red-shift from 537 to 566 nm, accompanied by a continuous change in the afterglow color from turquoise to deep yellow (Fig. 4a–d, S3 and Movie1-4). These phenomena are also caused by the strong charge transfer process resulting from CDs aggregation [51]. In order to further understand the phosphorescence properties of CDs@BA-(Ⅰ-Ⅳ), the RTP emission and lifetime decay profile spectra are also measured (Fig. 4e–h). The time-resolved phosphorescence decay spectra of these samples all exhibit a tri-exponential decay lifetime (Table S2). Based on the following equation [49]:
(1)
τ avg
= Σ
α i
τ i 2
/ Σ
α i
τ i

the RTP lifetimes of these products decrease from 664.7 to 338.3 ms, when the mass of CDs increases, demonstrating the concentration-dependent nature of the RTP emission. To further investigate the phosphorescence mechanism of CDs@BA composites, CDs@BA-I was selected as a representative sample for studying the effects of temperature on both phosphorescence emission and lifetime decay. The RTP intensity and lifetime of CDs@BA-I decreased significantly with increasing temperature, attributed to the slower radiative decay of long-lived excited states and enhanced non-radiative transitions at higher temperatures, indicating phosphorescence rather than delayed fluorescence (Fig. S6 and S7) [52]. The above results indicate that concentration-dependent CDs can not only modulate the FL emission by adjusting the concentration of the solution system but also provide the possibility of constructing color-tunable RTP composite materials by regulating the doping concentration of CDs. Considering the significance of material stability in practical applications, the stability of CDs@BA-I was evaluated. CDs@BA-I still retains a certain intensity of phosphorescence emission even after continuous exposure to UV light (365 nm, 50 W) for 12 h, indicating the certain photostability of the as prepared CDs@BA-I (Fig. S8).

The unique RTP characteristics of CDs@BA composites provide opportunities for the design of warning signs and some other interesting optical patterns. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the specific warning signs of safety exit and hazardous material can also be made to double warning with different optical colors outputs by UV on/off. The safety exit pattern made by CDs@BA-Ⅰ appears blue under 365 nm light excitation and then changes to turquoise after stopping excitation. While the hazardous goods pattern made by CDs@BA-Ⅲ appears gray (the color is a mixture of the powders themselves and the weak fluorescence emitted by the powders) under 365 nm light excitation and then changes to yellow after stopping excitation. In addition, when CDs@BA-Ⅲ powders and CDs aqueous solution (1.56 mg/mL) are placed in the shape of “I ♥ U”, “I” and “U” show gray color, and “♥” show blue color under the 365 nm UV light, respectively. However, when the UV light is turned off, the letters “I” and “U” still show a distinct yellow afterglow, while the shape “♥” no longer shows any optical color (Fig. 5b). This phenomenon indicates that “without light, there will be no love”.

In this study, we successfully synthesized CDs@BA composites exhibiting concentration-dependent and color-tunable RTA properties. The confinement of CDs within the BA matrix effectively suppressed non-radiative decay and stabilized triplet excitons, leading to enhanced afterglow performance. By varying the concentration of CDs, the afterglow emission could be tuned from turquoise to deep yellow. This work provides a novel strategy for designing carbon-based materials with colorful RTA, paving the way for the meaningful and interesting optical pattern design.

Jianliang Bai: Writing – original draft, Resources, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xuejia Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zidan Yin: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yanru Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Longlong Ma: Software, Funding acquisition. Mengzhao Li: Software. Cuixing Xu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Runfang Pei: Writing – review & editing. Lili Ren: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (202403021212048 and 202303021212275) and Scientific and Technological Innovation Program of Colleges and Universities in Shanxi Province (2023L328). The authors would like to thank the shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS tests.
